old_claim.note: Mercantilism Economic Theory provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Mercantilist colonial policy treated colonies as raw-material suppliers and export markets, commercially supporting tropical sugar plantations without being a technical production prerequisite.

source_shape.source_locator: Britannica describes mercantilism as a sixteenth- to eighteenth-century European system and says colonies in Africa and the Americas supplied raw materials to mother countries while buying finished goods from them.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: Mercantilist colonial economies used colonies as raw-material suppliers and controlled markets for European mother countries.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports the commercial-export context of plantation economies without making mercantilist theory a direct technical enabler of sugar cultivation, milling, or coerced labor organization.

ontology_before: The edge labeled mercantilism as an enabling capability for Plantation Economy, overstating an economic-policy context as a direct production enabler.
ontology_after: The edge now models mercantilism as commercial and scaling context for export plantations within colonial raw-material systems.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: mercantilism_economic_theory is now commercial_or_scaling_dependency. Preserved: the edge remains because the plantation node is explicitly scoped to colonial export agriculture.
would_reject_if: A source shows early São Tomé sugar estates emerged outside colonial export-market economics.
The plantation_economy node is later rescoped to only the physical sugar mill rather than the broader economic system.
short_reason: Mercantilism commercially frames colonial export plantations but does not technically enable them.
